coredata.dc:title: Multi-layer perceptron for detection of different class antibiotics from visual fluorescence response of a carbon nanoparticle-based multichannel array sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Mandal, Saptarshi
Paul, Dipanjyoti
Saha, Sriparna
Das, Prolay
coredata.dc:description: Lack of automated accurate decision-making along with an on-site detection system impedes the identification of substances of environmental concern. In pursuit of making this feasible, we interconnected our optical fluorescence array sensing strategy with the predictive analytics of artificial intelligence. Herein, we developed a Carbon Nanoparticle-based nine-channel fluorescence array sensing method for the detection of six antibiotics of different classes that are precariously dumped in the environment from various industrial and animal husbandry sources. The fluorescence responses of the arrays in the presence or absence of six antibiotics were captured digitally and these were utilized as feature values for the identification of classes using machine learning and deep learning algorithms. Among the seven tested multi-class classification algorithms, Multi-layer Perceptron (MLP) with Generative Adversarial Nets stimulated augmented data set (Aug-MLP) outdid the others in recognizing the antibiotics. Most importantly, the performance of Aug-MLP is comparable to fluorescence spectroscopic discrimination that outclasses human visual judgment. The whole methodology was found to adapt well in real samples like extracts of poultry feeds. In a nutshell, a nanotechnology-deep learning interfaced semi-automated on-site multi-class antibiotic detection strategy has been developed that could be extended for inexpensive and expedited detection of other chemical entities.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Machine learning
Antibiotic pollution
Chemical tongue
Optical sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Performing array sensing of the antibiotics

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) pXRD of CNPARS-pPD, disclosed partial graphitization. (B) TEM image of CNPARS-pPD revealed diameter mostly ~100 nm (Inset: Size distribution of CNPARS-pPD considering 50 particles from TEM images). (C) FTIR spectra of CNPARS-pPD showed the presence of diverse functional groups. (D) Zeta potential of CNPARS-pPD indicated a wide distribution of surface charge with peak centered at − 54.9 mV. (E) UV-Vis absorption spectrum of CNPARS-pPD range indicating various electronic transitions within CNPARS-pPD. (F) Steady-state fluorescence spectra of CNPARS-pPD showed excitation independent fluorescence emission signifying uniform surface states.
Fluorescence emission spectra of the six antibiotics along with the control (no antibiotics) at the excitation wavelength of 305 nm unveiled a distinct fluorescence pattern for all six antibiotics.
(A) The construction of MLP. (B) & (C) performance of various algorithms in terms of accuracy and f-measure. (D) The architecture of GANs.
Digital image of nine channels of CNPARS-pPD in water at pH 7.4 ± 0.1 with the antibiotics extracted from animal feed.
(A) Fluorescence spectroscopy-like discrimination by Aug-MLP. (B) Proposed mechanism for the distinct fluorescence response.

(A) Screening and optimization of the CNPs and metal ions. (B) Visual fluorescence response of the nine channels of optimized CNPARS-pPD in water at pH 7.4 ± 0.1. (C) Deep learning intervention for accurate identification of the antibiotics from CMYK inputs of digital images of the array sensing.
Identification of the antibiotics in poultry feed.

entry.bold: Aug-MLP identification before including spiked samples data in training set
Aug-MLP identification after including spiked samples data in training set

head.ce:title: Multi-layer perceptron for detection of different class antibiotics from visual fluorescence response of a carbon nanoparticle-based multichannel array sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, Patna 801103, Bihar, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Patna

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, Patna – 801103, Bihar, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Computer Science and Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, Patna 801103, Bihar, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Computer Science and Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Patna

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Computer Science and Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, Patna – 801103, Bihar, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lack of automated accurate decision-making along with an on-site detection system impedes the identification of substances of environmental concern. In pursuit of making this feasible, we interconnected our optical fluorescence array sensing strategy with the predictive analytics of artificial intelligence. Herein, we developed a Carbon Nanoparticle-based nine-channel fluorescence array sensing method for the detection of six antibiotics of different classes that are precariously dumped in the environment from various industrial and animal husbandry sources. The fluorescence responses of the arrays in the presence or absence of six antibiotics were captured digitally and these were utilized as feature values for the identification of classes using machine learning and deep learning algorithms. Among the seven tested multi-class classification algorithms, Multi-layer Perceptron (MLP) with Generative Adversarial Nets stimulated augmented data set (Aug-MLP) outdid the others in recognizing the antibiotics. Most importantly, the performance of Aug-MLP is comparable to fluorescence spectroscopic discrimination that outclasses human visual judgment. The whole methodology was found to adapt well in real samples like extracts of poultry feeds. In a nutshell, a nanotechnology-deep learning interfaced semi-automated on-site multi-class antibiotic detection strategy has been developed that could be extended for inexpensive and expedited detection of other chemical entities.

ce:section.ce:para: Around 80% of the total consumption of medically important antibiotics is used for the global animal sector. Unmistakably, this particular sector of the food industry has a lion’s share in antibiotic over usage and their subsequent unwarranted release in the environment leading to antibiotic resistance-related mortalities. Although World Health Organization (WHO) has specific guidelines for reduced usage of antibiotics in animal feeds, the onus is on us to monitor and detect their presence in the environment. Numerous analytical methods for the detection of single or multiple antibiotics have been reported such as colorimetric [1,2], fluorometric [3–6], electrochemical [7–9], Raman spectroscopy [10,11], immunochemical [12,13], etc. [14] However, in reality, there is no method for antibiotic detection for managing antibiotic pollution which is accessible to the general population. The reasons are mainly twofold; these methods mostly require sophisticated costly analytical instruments and such analytical strategies require the involvement of trained personnel. Although few point-of-care (POC) visual detection systems have been developed [15], these POC systems mostly use antibodies or aptamers as recognition elements and are constrained to detect single class of antibiotics. This naturally poses the question of whether we have an affordable single POC to detect multiple antibiotics, with unmatched shelf life and temperature tolerance with the provision for remote analysis. This delineates how a novel sensing platform is highly coveted for the detection of antibiotics in environmental samples yet achievable through a meaningful collaboration of multiple emerging fields, for eg. Nanotechnology and Artificial intelligence (AI).

Recently, CNPs have attracted tremendous attention for biosensing applications including fluorescent array-based sensing [16–20]. Easy and cheap synthesis, abundant precursors, excellent photoluminescence and photostability, biocompatibility are signature advantages of these CNPs [21]. Few CNPs-based methods were reported for the detection of single or even multiple antibiotics albeit using sophisticated analytical instruments [22–26]. These reports showed that the stability and functional groups abundance of CNPs could be advantageously used as recognition and signal generating elements which prompted us to develop a CNP-based visual detection system operable by untrained people through cloud data and remote sensing.

Identifying potent tools in both the fields and integrating them for a versatile strategic outcome is most significant rather than their random callouts. This is precisely achieved here, whereby the visual outcomes from the interactions of various antibiotics with a fluorescent Carbon Nanoparticle (CNP)-based multichannel array have been integrated with the concept of Generative Adversarial Nets (GAN). GANs is a powerful Deep Learning (DL) model that literally revolutionized image processing [27]. To the best of our knowledge, GANs have never been introduced in detecting chemical species before. Worthy mentioning, GAN was not arbitrarily used, rather introduced after an intense optimization exercise with as many as seven DL algorithms including Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) model that came out as the winner.

Detecting antibiotics in animal feeds is extremely complex for interferences from multiple additives present in them [28–30]. Reportedly, multidimensional or multi-channel sensing could detect antibiotics in different complex samples using several transduction channels, but based on data from sophisticated analytical instruments [6,31–33]. Consequently, the huge amount of data generated by a multidimensional array sensor required further intervention from traditional statistical pattern recognition methods like principal component analysis (PCA) and linear discriminant analysis (LDA) [34,35]. Being based on linear principles, compromised performance could happen for a multivariate data set. Here we conjectured the intervention of modern AI algorithms in the pattern recognition task to utilize its peerless advantages of reduced noise, enhanced specificity, better accuracy, fast readout, anomaly spotting, etc [36]. Encouragingly, various machine learning (ML) and DL algorithms wield their utility in bio-sensing systems for their abilities to find out hidden relationships among sample parameters and generate reasonable analytical outcomes from noisy, low-resolution data [36,37]. These are of foremost importance for a visual sensing strategy where no sophisticated analytical instrument is involved. However, among numerous algorithms, it cannot be anticipated which one will dominate over others on a particular data set. Hence, we have analyzed our array sensing data with seven supervised classification algorithms to find out the superior fit in the identification of the analytes accurately.

Today a digital camera is easily accessible to common people and this evoked researchers to use it as an analytical tool for detection technology [38–40]. Herein, we digitally captured the fluorescence responses under a UV-light source to optimize and develop the multi-channel fluorescent sensor array for the detection of six antibiotics of different classes relevant to animal feeds (β-lactams: Ampicillin (AMP), Quinolones: Ciprofloxacin (CPFX), Aminoglycosides: Kanamycin (KAN), Sulfonamides: Sulphamethoxazole (SMZ), Tetracyclines: Tetracycline (TET), and Diaminopyridines: Trimethoprim (TMP)) ( Scheme 1). For the first time, we used a DL-based method MLP for the detection of antibiotics which has been further compared with six other ML algorithms. Additionally, to further improve the performance of MLP, we took the identical image generation advantage of GANs. Overall, this work is an exclusive proof of concept of an inexpensive, fast yet accurate on-site AI boosted detection strategy where AI outperforms human visual identification by acting like a fluorescence spectroscopic discrimination.

CNPs were synthesized by solvothermal pyrolysis of small organic compounds. Briefly, 0.5 mmol of ARS and 0.5 mmol of p-phenylenediamine, were dissolved in 7 ML of water and transferred in a 10 ML hydrothermal autoclave. The autoclave was heated at 220 ℃ for 14 h. After cooling to room temperature, the crude mixture was filtered with a 0.22 µm syringe filter and then dialysed for 24 h using a 2 kDa dialysis membrane by occasionally changing the dialysate with fresh water. Five more CNPs from 0.5 mmol of ARS and 0.5 mmol of Cysteine or Histidine or Tris base or Tryptophan were also synthesized by following the same procedure (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performing array sensing of the antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para: Aqueous CNP dispersions (100 µg/µL) were made and pH was adjusted to 7.4 ± 0.1 with dilute NaOH solution. Stock antibiotic solutions (1 mM) were made with a water-ethanol (1:1) mixture. 100 mM stock metal ion solutions were made with water. For array sensing, antibiotics and metal ions were taken accordingly followed by the addition of 100 µL of CNP dispersion in each tube (0.5 ML) or vice-versa. The total volume in each tube was maintained as 110 µL. Water was used for making up the volume 110 µL whenever required. The fluorescence response of the CNPs under UV light irradiation on a UV transilluminator in different chemical environments was digitally captured.

To detect antibiotics in animal feeds by our array sensing strategy, poultry feed was collected from the local market. The feed was separately spiked with the six antibiotics. The antibiotics were extracted with a water-ethanol (7:3) mixture [30,41]. The extracted antibiotics were used in the array sensing following the same above-mentioned procedure.

An image is represented by a set of pixel values, where a pixel is the smallest element of an image. As an example, a 28 × 28 image has 28 × 28 = 784 pixel values. Here, we took each pixel value by four colours such as C (cyan), M (magenta), Y (yellow), and K (key) in the range of 0–100%. To create the data set from the images, CMYK values from 20 positions of a fluorescence digital response were considered and an average of all the 20 CYMK values was finally taken. The antibiotics were typified by differential fluorescence response of the nine-channel sensor array, i.e., each antibiotic was represented by thirty-six features in terms of ML. The entity corresponding to a particular antibiotic was obtained by testing with different amounts of antibiotics (from 1 nmol to 9 nmol) and all of them were considered to be part of the same class label. The total size of the original data set was 112. The whole data set was distributed into a training (85%) and a testing set (15%).

The KNN model considered the number of neighbors as 3 and used the Minkowski distance metric with each neighbor as uniform weightage. The method SVM used a radial basis function (rbf) kernel with a degree of 3. The GNB was implemented with a smoothing value of 1e-09. Whereas the optimizer used by the GPC was fmin_l_bfgs_b and maximum iteration of 100. RF used the gini-index to measure the quality of the split where the minimum sample split was taken as 2. The ABC was implemented considering the learning rate as 1.0 and the maximum number of estimators as 50. The MLP was a neural network-based approach consisting of 4 layers such as input, output, and 2 hidden layers. The hidden layers were of size 100 neurons. The ReLu activation function and adam optimizer with its default parameter values was used. The maximum number of epoch was considered as 200. Moreover, while generating the augmented data, the generator of the GAN network took the input from a random distribution of size 20. The generator and discriminator were feed-forward neural networks that consisted of three-layer of sizes 20, 50, and 36 for the generator and layer sizes of 36, 50, and 1 for the discriminator. It also used the ReLu activation function and adam optimizer.

Previously alizarin red S (ARS) was used for spectrophotometric determination of several drug molecules where non-covalent interactions between the ARS molecules and analytes played a key role [42–45]. This prompted us to keep ARS as a common precursor for the synthesis of a library of CNPs where the surface functionalization of CNPs was varied with other small organic molecules. Here, we used cysteine, histidine, p-phenylenediamine, tris base, or tryptophan as the second precursor (Table S1) to synthesize the CNP library. The six antibiotics could not be detected through a single channel fluorescent sensor array of CNPs (Table S2) prompting us to pick the optimum CNP for a multichannel fluorescent array sensing of antibiotics.

To create the multichannel, we prudently picked eight metal ions that are often been added to animal feedstuffs or present in relevant water sources [46,47]. Thus, we generated a nine-channel fluorescent array (blank, Na(I), Mg (II), K(I), Ca(II), Mn(II), Fe (III), Cu(II), Zn(II)) with a single CNP. With five different CNPs in the library, we created five separate nine-channel fluorescent sensor arrays. To all the fluorescent sensor arrays, antibiotics were added separately and digitally imaged under UV-B light irradiation (Fig. S1-S5). By scrutinizing the visual fluorescent responses, we then picked the CNPARS-pPD for further studies as the CNPARS-pPD was found to interact with the maximum number of antibiotics. To check the working possibilities in different buffer solutions, we tested the CNPARS-pPD based array sensing in 10 mM tris (pH 7.4) and 1X TTE (tris base, taurine, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid; pH 7.4) buffer also (Fig. S6-S7). However, the visual distinction of the antibiotics was found to decrease. We speculated that the components of the buffers crowded around the surface of CNPARS-pPD and thus hindered the access of the metal ions or antibiotic molecules towards the CNPARS-pPD.

In an anticipation of further improving the distinctiveness of the fluorescent array, we tested the antibiotics in four more metal ions (Al(III), Cr(III), Co(II), and Ni(II)) channels with the CNPARS-pPD (Fig. S8) under the same UV-B light irradiation. After scrutinizing the digitally captured image, we added the Al(III) channel and excluded Na(I) channel in further studies as part of the optimization process. Thus, the optimized nine channels are: blank-CNPARS-pPD, Mg(II)-CNPARS-pPD, Al(III)-CNPARS-pPD, K(I)-CNPARS-pPD, Ca(II)-CNPARS-pPD, Mn(II)-CNPARS-pPD, Fe(III)-CNPARS-pPD, Cu(II)-CNPARS-pPD, and Zn(II)-CNPARS-pPD.

Various morphological, physical, and chemical aspects of the CNPARS-pPD were characterized with a host of analytical techniques. Successful carbonization in CNPARS-pPD was evident with powder x-ray diffraction (pXRD) analysis ( Fig. 1 A), whereas shape (quasi-spherical) and size (~100 nm) of the CNPARS-pPD were obtained by transmission electron microscope (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) study (Fig. 1B, Fig. S9). The proton Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (1H NMR) revealed that a motley of aromatic fragments is present in the CNPARS-pPD (Fig. S10). The π-electrons present in those aromatic fragments provides a conducive environment to interact with the metal cations and antibiotics. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopic study showed that amine, hydroxyl, keto, and sulfonate functional groups from the precursors were partially translated in the CNPARS-pPD (Fig. 1 C). These surface functional groups create the basis of non-covalent interactions in different ion channels created hereafter. The mean zeta potential of CNPARS-pPD was measured as − 53.9 ± 2.6 mV indicating abundancy of negatively charged functional groups on the CNPARS-pPD surface (Fig. 1D). Electronic transitions in CNPARS-pPD were disclosed by the UV-Vis absorption spectroscopy, where the peaks at 260 nm, 325 nm, and 540 nm are corresponding to π-π * transitions at the core, n-π * transitions at the edge and transitions associated with the surface functional groups respectively (Fig. 1E) [48,49]. The steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy revealed excitation independent red-region fluorescence emission of the CNPARS-pPD where the excitation maxima are at wavelength 560 nm and emission maxima are at wavelength 650 nm (Fig. 1 F).

The fluorescence response of the nine-channel array in presence of different amounts of the six antibiotics (from 1 nmol to 8 nmol) was digitally captured to extract the CMYK values for downstream processing with the ML algorithms (Fig. S11-S14). In an anticipation of increasing compliance of our array sensing strategy to a real detection scenario, we repeated the same above processes with a higher amount of individual cations, and lower amounts of mixed cations, and antibiotics (Fig. S15-S18). Unfortunately, despite all the variations we could only visually discriminate ciprofloxacin and tetracycline from the fluorescent sensor array. However, the discrimination was possible with fluorescence spectroscopy. We measured the fluorescence spectra of the nine-channel array in presence and absence of the antibiotics in a 96 well plate reader at an excitation wavelength of 305 nm ( Fig. 2). Encouragingly, here we witnessed a distinct pattern for all six antibiotics. This happened due to differential interactions among CNPARS-pPD, metal ions, and antibiotics [32,50]. Additionally, the native blue fluorescence for ciprofloxacin suppresses the peaks for CNPARS-pPD at higher wavelengths and puts an additive effect to the peaks at lower wavelengths.

Observing the fluorescent spectroscopic discrimination of antibiotics, we set out to explore if ML or DL algorithms could spot the hidden differences in the digital images of the CNP array to identify the six different classes of antibiotics without using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. Since here the outputs are the antibiotics that are predefined or labelled, we have used supervised classification algorithms. It trains a model with a set of input-output variables to infer a mapping function based on which the class label of unseen data is predicted. Here, we considered each antibiotic as a separate class label irrespective of their amount in the array sensor and the array without any antibiotic (control) as another class label, i.e., a total of seven class labels. Each entity was represented by CNPARS-pPD based on nine separate channels which are cited as the features of an entity. The whole data set was generated by extracting the CMYK values from the digitally captured fluorescence responses of each antibiotic-nine channels of CNPARS-pPD along with the control (without any antibiotic) which was divided into a separate training and testing set to forefend any potential overfitting.

Since it cannot be predicted for a given set of data which particular algorithm will prevail over others, our primary job was to find out the best-performing algorithm. For this, we have employed seven classification algorithms: K-nearest Neighbour (KNN), Support-vector Machine (SVM), Gaussian Naive Bayes (GNB), Multi-layer Perceptron (MLP), Global Product Classifier (GPC), Random Forest (RF), and Artificial Bee Colony (ABC). Only MLP, which is a deep-based algorithm ( Fig. 3 A), scored above 0.80 (accuracy 0.82, f-measure 0.81), whereas the rest of the algorithms were unable to score above 0.55 (Fig. 3B-C). This delineates the supremacy of the DL method over the ML methods in the discrimination task. However, the data set that is derived from non-high throughput experimental findings is generally small, as in the present case. For this reason, we could not employ other DL algorithms in this job that primarily require a huge amount of data set. But, the small amount of data generates a scope of additional intellection. GANs have achieved extensive success in image processing and computer vision even without a large amount of training data [27,51]. Here, we hypothesize that GANs could be used the same way to improve the performance of the computer models in the detection of different classes of antibiotics. GANs (Fig. 3D) was used to extend the total data set by adding some real-alike (fake) samples (that follow the same distribution as the original) in the training data set. The generator (G) of GANs grabbed the label information and noise as the input to generate data corresponding to a class label. The discriminator (D) then judge that generated data as real or fake and accordingly both the G and D networks train themselves by trying to fool each other. By this approach, we were able to improve the performance of the algorithms by 0.01–0.03 and unsurprisingly Aug-MLP came out as the best with an accuracy of 0.84 and an f-measure score of 0.83. The Aug-MLP model was thus preferred to do the identification task in a real scenario such as antibiotics in extracts of poultry feeds.

The functionality of the whole sensing strategy was appraised with poultry feed spiked with antibiotics. Similar to the pure antibiotic samples, the visual fluorescence response of the nine channels of CNPARS-pPD in presence and absence of the poultry feed extracted antibiotics was captured digitally ( Fig. 4). The corresponding CMYK values were extracted for testing with the Aug-MLP model. The model accurately identified four out of six antibiotics whereas visually we could detect only two antibiotics ( Table 1). Here, the performance reduction of Aug-MLP may be due to interferences that have come from the poultry feeds. Then, we included a set of poultry feed data to the training phase of the Aug-MLP and tested it with another round of poultry feed data. This time the performance of the Aug-MLP matched that with the pure antibiotics and more encouragingly, it did not render any cross identification similar to the previous assessment of poultry feed antibiotics. This indicates the importance of including data of different detection environments in the training set as well as the high adaptability of our Aug-MLP generated model to a real detection scenario. Optimistically, we ideate that by creating a better differential chemical environment through nano-fabrication, exerting a large number of data set including different real sensing environments, finding a better algorithm, and wielding a better image transforming method, could attend ~100% performance in identifying multi-class antibiotics through an ML or DL model.

Thus a detection strategy of different classes of antibiotics was demonstrated where no sophisticated analytical instruments were involved and that could be performed by a non-technical person also. Although the methodology demonstrated here is for the qualitative detection of different classes of antibiotics, the fluorescent array sensor could produce discriminable data at a few nmol amounts of antibiotics. Reaching an extraordinary limit of detection of the antibiotics is never the intention of this study, instead, our intention is to explore novel ML and DL approaches towards visual sensing of the analytes whose relevant concentration in environmental samples could be easily detected just using a digital image of the array. Most importantly, the predictive analytics of AI or the accurate discrimination power of DL by probing the invisible relationships among the sample parameters come out nearly full mooned to outstrip the human visual discernment and act similar to fluorescence spectroscopy ( Fig. 5A). Although precisely establishing the sensing mechanism is not the intent of this array sensing strategy, we can say that change in the surrounding charge environment of the CNPARS-pPD is responsible here for differential fluorescence response of the nine-channel array (Fig. 5B) [50]. When the fluorescence spectra were measured at an excitation wavelength of 560 nm, no distinctive fluorescence pattern was observed for different antibiotics used here (Fig. S19). It indicates that the edge state of the CNPARS-pPD (n-π * transition) is responsible here for the differential fluorescence behaviour of the nine-channel array [52].

Developing a POC system with automated decision-making is the supreme quest in detecting any analyte. Towards this, we have successfully made a marriage between Nano-chemistry and AI in the form of multi-class antibiotics detection that primarily addresses the global concern of rampant usage of antibiotics in animal feeds. Herein, our primary objective and motive were to develop an AI boosted inexpensive, rapid, unbiased, semi-automated on-site detection scheme for various classes of antibiotics that could be handled even by common personnel. In this process, optimization of the CNP array is most critical to obtain discernible visual fluorescence responses under UV light. The fluorescence of CNPs was taken to an advantage to construct a nine-channel array sensor along with eight cations. We first screened out the optimum CNP and then added Al (III) channel in a quest of improving the distinctiveness in the pattern. The fluorescent array does not require additional recognition elements such as an antibody or an aptamer, and conceptually mimics the art of a human tongue. However, even after all those optimization procedures, visual discrimination was attained for two antibiotics only, although all the antibiotics are quite discernible from their fluorescent spectroscopic pattern. Finally, we used ML/DL algorithms where the data was digitally captured fluorescent images, not the fluorescent spectrophotometer generated data. The digital images of the fluorescence response of the nine-channel array in presence of six different classes of antibiotics under UV-B light irradiation were subsequently converted into the array sensor data to feed the seven multi-class classification algorithms where MLP, a deep-based supervised algorithm came out superior. Most importantly, the antibiotic discrimination power of the MLP not only overtakes a human visual judgment but also is comparable to a fluorescent spectrophotometric response. In pursuit of enhancing the performance of the algorithms further, we implemented the data augmentation power of the GANs, where Aug-MLP outperformed others. We tested our methodology with poultry feed spiked antibiotics and its successful outcomes delineate the high adaptability of our detection strategy to a realistic detection environment. Needless to mention, such a test could be performed by an untrained person outside a laboratory set-up with possible real-time transmission of test results. Overall, it is a unique proof of concept of the authority of DL in a discrimination task that is comparable to a highly sophisticated spectrophotometer. In the future, the array sensor images can directly be used for classification using DL algorithms like Convolution Neural Networks and for quantitative detection also. Thus, this work is a giant step forward towards the development of a semi-automated DL-based optical array sensor that could be transformed into a POC kit under 1 USD for general public use.

Saptarshi Mandal: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – Original draft, Visualization. Dipanjyoti Paul: Methodology, Software, Validation, Data curation, Writing – Original draft. Dr. Sriparna Saha: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision. Dr. Prolay Das: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - Review & Editing, Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: P.D. and S.S. thank the Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Patna for infrastructural and financial assistance. S.M. and D.P. thank IIT Patna for Institute Research Fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131660.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Colorimetric sensor arrays for the detection and identification of antibiotics
Anal. Methods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.A. Abedalwafa, Y. Li, C. Ni, L. Wang, Colorimetric sensor arrays for the detection and identification of antibiotics, Anal. Methods. 11 (2019) 2836–2854. doi:10.1039/c9ay00371a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive and selective colorimetric sensing of antibiotics in milk
Anal. Chim. Acta

Curr. Opin. Biomed. Eng.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Anionic metal-organic framework as a unique turn-on fluorescent chemical sensor for ultra-sensitive detection of antibiotics
Chem. Commun.

Talanta

Zhao, Lanthanide-functionalized metal-organic frameworks based ratiometric fluorescent sensor array for identification and determination of antibiotics, Talanta. 231 (2021) 122366. doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2021.122366.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fingerprinting antibiotics with PAE-based fluorescent sensor arrays

ACS Sens.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Saptarshi Mandal is currently pursuing his Ph.D. degree in the Department of Chemistry, IIT Patna. Previously, he has received his MTech. degree in Pharmaceutical and Fine Chemical Technology in 2017 and post-B.Sc. B.Tech. degree in Chemical Technology in 2015 from the University of Calcutta. His B.Sc. in Chemistry (Hons.) degree is from West Bengal State University in 2012. He was selected for the Newton Bhabha PhD placement programme for the year 2019–20. His current research focuses on carbon dots and biomolecules for various biomedical applications.

Dipanjyoti Paul received the M.Tech degree in the Department of Information Technology from the Indian Institute of Engineering Science & Technology, Shibpur in the year 2016. Currently, he is a senior research scholar in the Department of computer science & engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Patna. He has publications in premier journals and conferences such as PR, ESWA, PRL, TKDD. KBS, GECCO, ASONAM, ICONIP etc. His research interests include subspace clustering, multiobjective optimization, deep learning, streaming feature selection, multi-label data, streaming data clustering, online research topic recommendation system, extractive online summarization. He is a student member of the IEEE.

Dr. Sriparna Saha received her Ph.D. degree in computer science from Indian Statistical Institute, Kolkata, India in 2011, respectively. She is currently an Associate Professor in the Department of Computer Science and Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, India. She has authored or coauthored more than 290 papers that fetched 5221 citations with H index 30. Her current research interests include deep learning, natural language processing, machine learning, information extraction, text mining, bioinformatics, and multiobjective optimization. She is also a senior member of IEEE. She is the recipient of the Google India Women in Engineering Award, 2008, DUO-India fellowship 2020, Humboldt Research Fellowship, Indo-U.S. Fellowship for Women in STEMM (WISTEMM) Women Overseas Fellowship program 2018 and CNRS fellowship. She had also received India4EU fellowship of European Union for Postdoctoral Research at University of Trento, Italy. She has received Erasmus Mundus Mobility with Asia (EMMA) fellowship of European Union at Heidelberg University, Germany. She had worked as visiting scientist at University of Caen, France, and University of Kyoto, Japan. She is Associate Editor of IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems, Neurocomputing (Elsevier), IEEE/ACM Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics, Expert Systems with Applications, and academic editor of PLOS ONE.

Professor Prolay Das obtained his B. Sc. degree from Presidency College, Calcutta University, M. Sc from IIT Bombay, and Ph.D. in Biochemistry from Georgia Institute of Technology, Atlanta, USA. He was a NASA and DAE funded Post-Doctoral fellow at the Biology Department, Brookhaven National Laboratory, New York, USA. He joined IIT Patna in 2010 as Assistant Professor and is currently a Professor there. His research interest is in Carbon Dots and DNA nanotechnology.
